name: Jerusalem
description: The core city of biblical worship, kingship, exile memory, and the New Testament Passion and early church narratives.
excavationSummary: Modern excavations have documented layered urban occupation across multiple biblical periods, especially in and around the City of David and the southern ridge.
historicalSignificance: Jerusalem is the main theological and political center of the biblical story and the most archaeologically layered city in the dataset.
caution: The city is secure, but the size of ancient phases and the interpretation of individual structures remain debated.

name: Jericho
description: An oasis settlement at Tell es-Sultan and one of the oldest continuously studied sites in the Levant.
excavationSummary: Excavations have produced long occupation sequences, though the date and identification of destruction layers remain debated in biblical archaeology.
historicalSignificance: Jericho is important for understanding settlement patterns in the Jordan Valley and the relationship between text, tradition, and stratigraphy.
caution: The archaeological site is secure, but the biblical conquest chronology is a separate scholarly question.

name: Megiddo
description: A major tell guarding the Jezreel Valley with dense occupation and repeated military significance.
excavationSummary: Long-running excavations have made Megiddo one of the most important type sites for Levantine stratigraphy.
historicalSignificance: Megiddo anchors broad historical study because its strata help correlate biblical, Assyrian, and regional chronology.
caution: Individual strata and the interpretation of some features remain debated even though the site itself is secure.

name: Hazor
description: A major northern city and administrative center with impressive Bronze and Iron Age remains.
excavationSummary: Excavations have shown a long and complex occupation history, including destruction horizons often discussed in biblical studies.
historicalSignificance: Hazor is crucial for understanding northern Canaanite and Israelite urbanism.
caution: The city is not in doubt, but dating specific destruction levels remains a live scholarly discussion.

name: Lachish
description: A strategically important Judahite city famous for its siege history and monumental remains.
excavationSummary: Excavations have documented successive fortification phases and a dramatic destruction horizon associated with Assyrian campaigns.
historicalSignificance: Lachish is one of the clearest archaeological windows into Judah's final centuries.
caution: The site is secure, though details of the Assyrian siege sequence are still reconstructed from multiple lines of evidence.

name: Gezer
description: A border city near the coastal plain and hill country with strong Bronze and Iron Age significance.
excavationSummary: Excavations have revealed a long occupation history and major Iron Age architecture associated with royal administrative concerns.
historicalSignificance: Gezer helps illuminate border control, agricultural life, and royal building programs.
caution: The city is clear, but interpretations of some Iron Age structures continue to vary.

name: Beth Shean
description: A major settlement in the Jordan Valley with impressive Egyptian, Israelite, and Roman remains.
excavationSummary: The site preserves layered occupation from Egyptian administration through the Roman city of Scythopolis.
historicalSignificance: Beth Shean shows how a major regional center changed across imperial transitions.
caution: The tell and the Roman city are both real, but biblical references span different occupation phases.

name: Capernaum
description: A lakeside town central to the Galilean ministry narratives in the Gospels.
excavationSummary: Excavations have documented a first-century Jewish settlement and later Byzantine-Christian memory layers.
historicalSignificance: Capernaum is one of the clearest New Testament localities in the archaeological record.
caution: The town is secure, but specific Gospel episodes should not be treated as archaeologically proven by the site alone.

name: Nazareth
description: A Galilean village associated with the childhood of Jesus and early Christian memory.
excavationSummary: Archaeology shows a small settlement in the relevant period, while later traditions expanded the site's importance.
historicalSignificance: Nazareth helps frame the social and geographic setting of the Gospels.
caution: The settlement is real, but the precise early footprint and all later traditional claims remain debated.

name: Magdala
description: A lakeside town with important Second Temple period remains and a strong New Testament-era profile.
excavationSummary: Recent excavations brought to light a well-preserved Jewish town with major ritual and domestic architecture.
historicalSignificance: Magdala expands the archaeological picture of Jewish life around the Sea of Galilee.
caution: The settlement is clear, but direct links to named Gospel episodes remain inferential.

name: Caesarea Maritima
description: Herod's harbor city and a major Roman administrative center in Acts.
excavationSummary: Excavations have revealed the harbor complex, urban infrastructure, and the famous Latin inscription naming Pontius Pilate.
historicalSignificance: Caesarea is a key site for Roman provincial administration and early Christian mission history.
caution: The city is secure, but not every structure visible today dates to the same phase.

name: Qumran
description: A settlement and cave network associated with the Dead Sea Scrolls discovery zone.
excavationSummary: Excavation and manuscript recovery produced one of the most important textual discoveries in biblical studies.
historicalSignificance: Qumran connects archaeology, textual transmission, and Second Temple Judaism.
caution: The scroll discovery is secure, but the identity of the settlement's inhabitants remains debated.

name: Mount Nebo
description: The traditional viewing point associated with Moses' final gaze toward the promised land.
excavationSummary: Archaeology documents a long pilgrimage history, especially in the Byzantine period and later memorial development.
historicalSignificance: Mount Nebo is important as a memory site where tradition and archaeology intersect.
caution: The mountain is real, but its biblical identification depends mainly on tradition.

name: Madaba
description: A town famous for its mosaic heritage and the Madaba Map tradition.
excavationSummary: Urban archaeology and mosaic traditions document the town's long Christian and post-Christian history.
historicalSignificance: Madaba is especially valuable for its late antique cartographic and pilgrimage evidence.
caution: The town is secure, but specific biblical associations are broader than one excavation horizon.

name: Pi-Ramesses
description: A Ramesside royal capital identified with the Qantir area in the eastern Delta.
excavationSummary: Work in the Delta identified the most likely location of the Ramesside capital, though the Exodus chronology remains disputed.
historicalSignificance: Pi-Ramesses is important for reconstructing late Bronze Age Egyptian imperial power.
caution: The capital's location is broadly accepted, but connecting it to the Exodus narrative is a separate question.

name: Elephantine
description: A Nile island with major Jewish papyri and a long sequence of cultic and administrative remains.
excavationSummary: Excavations and document finds revealed a Jewish military colony and a rich archive of daily life in Persian-period Egypt.
historicalSignificance: Elephantine is a key witness to diaspora Judaism and imperial administration.
caution: The site is secure, but the biblical connections are indirect rather than narrative-specific.

name: Babylon
description: The great Mesopotamian capital central to exile, empire, and later symbolic readings of Scripture.
excavationSummary: Excavations at Babylon and its surrounding mound complex have documented the imperial city behind the biblical exile setting.
historicalSignificance: Babylon is indispensable for understanding exile, imperial administration, and prophetic memory.
caution: The city is secure, though biblical references sometimes compress several imperial phases.

name: Nineveh
description: The Assyrian capital associated with Jonah and the empire that shaped much of the Old Testament narrative world.
excavationSummary: Excavations have revealed palace complexes, text libraries, and monumental city defenses from the Assyrian imperial center.
historicalSignificance: Nineveh is essential for studying Assyrian politics, art, and biblical prophetic critique.
caution: The city is secure, but some biblical narratives compress complex imperial history into literary scenes.

name: Ur
description: A major southern Mesopotamian city associated with Abraham's departure tradition.
excavationSummary: Excavations established Ur as one of the best known cities of southern Mesopotamia, though its link to Abraham is traditional rather than demonstrable.
historicalSignificance: Ur provides the Mesopotamian backdrop for patriarchal memory and early urban life.
caution: The city is securely identified, but the Abrahamic association is not archaeologically provable.

name: Ephesus
description: A major Greco-Roman city and important center for Pauline mission and later Christian memory.
excavationSummary: Excavation has revealed an extensive Roman urban fabric and major public monuments that illuminate the New Testament setting.
historicalSignificance: Ephesus is one of the best studied cities in the New Testament world.
caution: The city is secure, but the harbor landscape changed over time and some locations are reconstructed.

name: Pergamum
description: A powerful Hellenistic and Roman center noted in Revelation and for its dramatic acropolis setting.
excavationSummary: Excavations have documented the acropolis complex and the urban footprint of the ancient city.
historicalSignificance: Pergamum helps explain the cultural and political pressures behind the letters to the seven churches.
caution: The city is certain, but some monument identifications are more interpretive than conclusive.

name: Laodicea
description: A wealthy inland city addressed in Revelation and known for substantial Roman remains.
excavationSummary: Modern excavation has exposed the urban layout and monumental architecture of the Roman city.
historicalSignificance: Laodicea is valuable for understanding late first-century urban Christianity in Asia Minor.
caution: The city is secure, though the Revelation passage is theological literature rather than a site description.
